items.description: Large, flourished thorn (þ) with looped ascender and calligraphic form extending into the text column as a decorated initial.
items.scholarly_note: Standard sententia or capitulum marker in 13th-c. Icelandic manuscripts; indicates start of new textual unit.

items.description: Second large thorn (þ) initial marking a subsequent section or stanza.
items.scholarly_note: Functional text-structuring device; consistent with the manuscript's ordinary practice.

items.description: Large capital G (or Q-like form) with elongated curved descender and flourishes beginning the final visible paragraph.
items.scholarly_note: Marks a new narrative or stanza division; the form is more elaborate than the thorns above.

items.description: Irregular tear with substantial parchment loss; upper portions of the first two lines and the extreme top-left corner are missing or illegible.
items.scholarly_note: Typical edge damage from handling and previous bindings; partial letters ('mæ' or similar) survive above the main block.

items.description: Dark brown-black irregular stain, possibly ink bleed combined with moisture, darkening parchment and slightly affecting the opening lines.
items.scholarly_note: Common in vellum codices; does not appear to be modern.

items.region: lower-right quadrant, bottom margin, and right edge
items.description: Extensive mottled brownish discoloration with surface darkening; ink in affected lines appears slightly faded or bled.
items.scholarly_note: Consistent with water or binding-related staining; heaviest concentration at bottom-right corner.

items.region: lower third of page, center to right
items.description: Broad zone of surface abrasion and raised parchment nap; multiple words show ink loss or blurring from rubbing.
items.scholarly_note: Mechanical wear, possibly from dry fingers or contact with other leaves; no clear deliberate erasure.

items.description: Ragged, worn edge with corner loss and heavy discoloration; parchment is thin and translucent here.
items.scholarly_note: Cumulative handling damage; text at foot remains mostly legible but compromised.

items.region: extreme top margin / head of page
items.description: Trimmed or misaligned head; faint partial letters visible above the ruled text block.
items.scholarly_note: Evidence of later rebinding or edge trimming of the codex.

scratches: Fine horizontal and diagonal abrasions across lower text block (approx. lines 12-20), causing localized ink flaking.
Narrow vertical scratch-like abrasion near right margin, mid-lower page, affecting two or three letters.
Diffuse micro-abrasions creating a mottled, lighter surface tone in the bottom-right quadrant.
damage_notes: Significant material loss along entire top edge and especially top-left corner.
Worn, darkened, and thinned bottom-right corner and fore-edge.
General surface grime, foxing, and age-related discoloration across recto.
Parchment thin and translucent in heavily handled zones; no active biological damage (mold, insects) clearly visible.
No modern repairs, patches, or conservation interventions observed on this image.
summary: Page 11 of Codex Regius shows moderate-to-heavy use-wear typical of a consulted 13th-century Icelandic manuscript. Three functional marginal initials (two thorns, one large G) structure the text. Dominant damage consists of top-edge tearing with text loss, moisture stains (upper-left and lower-right), and abrasion in the lower third. No doodles, pen trials, or unambiguous scribal corrections are visible. The main text body remains substantially legible despite the physical degradation.
